name: Japanese Onmyodo
description: Onmyodo is a traditional Japanese esoteric cosmology, a system of natural science, astronomy, divination, and magic. It originated in China, based on the philosophies of Wu Xing (the Five Elements) and Yin and Yang. Introduced to Japan in the 6th century, it was integrated with native Shinto beliefs and practices, eventually becoming a distinct system. Onmyodo practitioners, known as Onmyoji, were skilled in divination, exorcism, geomancy, and calendar-making. They served the imperial court and later, the shogunate, advising on matters of state and personal fortune. Their powers were believed to influence natural phenomena and ward off evil spirits. Key aspects include the manipulation of shikigami (familiar spirits), the use of Ofuda (talismans), and the performance of rituals to maintain balance and harmony. While its official influence waned after the Meiji Restoration, Onmyodo continues to influence Japanese culture through folklore, literature, and popular media. The practice emphasizes understanding and harmonizing with the natural world to achieve desired outcomes and maintain spiritual well-being. Onmyodo aims to control and manipulate the flow of ki, or life energy, to influence events and protect individuals from harm, blending Chinese cosmology with indigenous Japanese spiritual traditions.
subtitle: Esoteric Cosmology and Spiritual Practice

keyMyths.title: Abe no Seimei's Origins
keyMyths.description: Abe no Seimei, a legendary Onmyoji, is often depicted as having supernatural parentage, with a Kitsune (fox spirit) mother. This origin story highlights his innate connection to the spiritual realm and his exceptional magical abilities.

sources.author: Carmen Blacker
sources.description: Explores Japanese shamanism and its relation to Onmyodo.
sources.text: Explores Japanese shamanism and its relation to Onmyodo.

sources.author: Richard Cavendish
sources.description: General overview of Onmyodo within a broader encyclopedia of mythology.
sources.text: General overview of Onmyodo within a broader encyclopedia of mythology.

sources.author: Micheal Como
sources.work: Weaving and Binding: Immigrant Gods and Female Shamans in Ancient Japan
sources.description: Examines the historical roots of shamanistic practices that influenced Onmyodo.
sources.text: Examines the historical roots of shamanistic practices that influenced Onmyodo.

worshipPractices.name: Ritual Cleansing
worshipPractices.description: Purification rituals are central to Onmyodo, aimed at removing negative energy or malevolent spirits. These rituals often involve the use of salt, water, and incantations to cleanse spaces and individuals. The goal is to restore balance and harmony according to Yin and Yang principles.

worshipPractices.name: Divination
worshipPractices.description: Onmyoji employ various divination techniques to predict the future, assess auspicious timings, and diagnose spiritual ailments. These methods can include astrology, geomancy, and interpreting omens to gain insight into unseen forces. Divination helps in making informed decisions and avoiding misfortune.

festivals.name: Setsubun
festivals.description: While not exclusively an Onmyodo festival, Setsubun incorporates elements of exorcism and purification. People throw roasted soybeans while chanting "Oni wa soto! Fuku wa uchi!" (Demons out! Fortune in!) to drive away evil spirits and invite good luck for the coming year, reflecting the Onmyodo desire for balance.

festivals.name: Doyo Period Observances
festivals.timing: Four times a year, lasting about 18 days each
festivals.description: Doyo periods are considered unlucky, and Onmyodo principles influence how people act during this time. Avoidance of major construction, travel, and starting new ventures is common, as these periods are believed to be when the earth is unstable and susceptible to negative energies.

cultural.modernLegacy: Onmyodo's influence persists in contemporary Japanese culture through literature, film, anime, and manga, often portraying Onmyoji as powerful spiritual figures. The aesthetic and symbolism of Onmyodo, particularly the use of talismans and complex geometric patterns, continue to fascinate and inspire artistic expression. Moreover, aspects of feng shui and traditional healing practices are often linked back to Onmyodo principles of harmony and balance.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Seiðr, like Onmyodo, involved divination, spellcasting, and working with spirits to influence events and see the future. Both systems were used for both benevolent and malevolent purposes.
Hermeticism, like Onmyodo, encompasses astrology, alchemy, and the manipulation of natural forces. Both systems aim to understand and control the universe through esoteric knowledge.

Heka, the Egyptian concept of magic, was a fundamental force that permeated the universe, much like the principles underlying Onmyodo. Both systems involved rituals, incantations, and the manipulation of divine power to achieve desired outcomes.

Druids, like practitioners of Onmyodo, were deeply connected to nature and utilized divination to see the future. They practiced rituals and worked with elements to influence events and maintain balance.

associations.name: Wood
associations.significance: Represents growth and vitality, vital aspects of Onmyodo's influence on prosperity and health.

associations.name: The North Star
associations.significance: Serves as a guiding point and represents the balance between yin and yang, reflecting Onmyodo's role in harmonizing energies.

associations.name: Balance
associations.significance: Onmyodo seeks to balance yin and yang, the five elements, and the natural world to achieve harmony.

associations.name: Kitsune (Fox)
associations.significance: Often associated with magic and cunning, mirroring the strategic and insightful aspects of Onmyodo practices.

associations.name: Purple
associations.significance: Historically associated with nobility and spiritual power, reflecting Onmyodo's influence within the imperial court.

associations.name: Five
associations.significance: Symbolizes the Wu Xing (Five Elements) which form a foundational aspect of Onmyodo's cosmological framework.

associations.name: Bamboo
associations.significance: Represents resilience and flexibility, echoing the adaptable nature of Onmyodo as it integrates diverse beliefs.

companions.name: Kuzunoha
companions.description: A kitsune (fox spirit) disguised as a woman, she is known for her intelligence and magical abilities, often assisting Onmyoji with their tasks. She can provide guidance and perform illusions.

companions.name: Zenki & Goki
companions.description: Powerful oni (demons) tamed and bound to serve the Onmyoji, acting as protectors and enforcers of their will. They assist with banishing evil spirits and carrying out difficult tasks.

companions.name: Abe no Seimei
companions.description: The most famous Onmyoji in Japanese history. He is an incredibly powerful and wise ally who would mentor and assist with particularly difficult challenges.

companions.name: Shikigami
companions.description: Spirits summoned and controlled by Onmyoji to perform various tasks, from simple chores to combat. They are bound by contracts and obey the commands of their master.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Development of Onmyodo
extendedContent.content:
Onmyodo's roots lie deeply embedded in ancient Chinese philosophies, primarily the Wu Xing (Five Elements: Wood, Fire, Earth, Metal, Water) and Yin-Yang principles. These concepts, alongside cosmological ideas related to astrology and divination, were imported to Japan from China, mainly through the Korean peninsula, during the 6th and 7th centuries. Initially, it was absorbed into the existing courtly structure and used for practical purposes such as calendar making, astronomical observation, and divining auspicious dates for imperial ceremonies. The *Nihon Shoki* (Chronicles of Japan), one of the earliest historical records, mentions practitioners of similar arts, indicating the early integration of continental practices. Over time, Onmyodo underwent a significant transformation in Japan, influenced by indigenous Shinto beliefs and local folklore. This fusion resulted in a uniquely Japanese system, distinct from its Chinese predecessor, with its own pantheon of spirits and deities, and a focus on pacifying malevolent forces and ensuring harmony between the human and spiritual realms. 

The establishment of the Onmyoryo, the Bureau of Onmyo, within the Imperial Court during the Heian period (794-1185) solidified Onmyodo's status as an official institution. This bureau was responsible for managing astronomical observations, calendar production, and divination for the Imperial family and the court. The head of the Onmyoryo held considerable power and influence, often advising the Emperor on matters of state and personal affairs. The prominence of figures like Abe no Seimei, a legendary Onmyoji, further cemented Onmyodo's place in Japanese society and popular imagination. His life, shrouded in myth and legend, became the subject of numerous stories and plays, portraying him as a master of magic and divination, capable of controlling spirits and manipulating the elements. 

The development of Onmyodo was not without its challenges. Throughout its history, it faced competition from other religious and spiritual traditions, including Buddhism and Shinto. However, its association with the Imperial Court and its perceived ability to influence fate and fortune ensured its continued survival and relevance. The Kamakura and Muromachi periods witnessed a decline in the central authority of the Onmyoryo, leading to the rise of independent Onmyoji who practiced their craft outside of the court's control. This decentralization resulted in a diversification of Onmyodo practices and the emergence of regional variations.

extendedContent.title: Key Principles and Practices
extendedContent.content:
At the heart of Onmyodo lies the principle of achieving harmony and balance between opposing forces, particularly Yin and Yang. Yin represents the passive, dark, and feminine aspects of the universe, while Yang embodies the active, light, and masculine. Onmyodo practitioners sought to understand and manipulate these forces to influence various aspects of life, from health and prosperity to protection from evil spirits. The Five Elements (Wu Xing) also play a crucial role, representing the fundamental building blocks of the universe and their interactions. Each element is associated with specific colors, directions, seasons, and emotions, and understanding these associations was essential for performing effective rituals and divinations. 

Divination was a central practice in Onmyodo, employed to predict future events, identify auspicious dates, and diagnose the causes of misfortune. Onmyoji used various methods, including astrology, geomancy, and the interpretation of dreams and omens, to gain insight into the unseen world. Another crucial aspect of Onmyodo involved the manipulation of spirits and deities. Onmyoji were believed to possess the ability to summon and control various spirits, both benevolent and malevolent, to achieve their desired outcomes. This often involved performing elaborate rituals, chanting incantations, and using talismans and amulets to ward off evil influences. The use of shikigami, familiars or spirits bound to the Onmyoji, became a common trope in the later legends surrounding Onmyodo. These entities acted as extensions of the Onmyoji's will, carrying out tasks such as spying, defending their master, or even attacking enemies.

Purification rituals were also an integral part of Onmyodo practice. These rituals were designed to cleanse individuals and spaces of negative energy and spiritual impurities. Various methods were employed, including the use of salt, water, and fire, as well as the recitation of specific prayers and mantras. Onmyoji were often called upon to perform exorcisms to rid individuals of demonic possession or to purify haunted locations. The use of ofuda, paper amulets inscribed with sacred symbols and prayers, was another common practice to ward off evil and attract good fortune. These amulets were often placed in homes, temples, and other sacred spaces to create a protective barrier against negative influences.

extendedContent.title: Stories and Legends of Onmyoji
extendedContent.content:
The figure of Abe no Seimei (921-1005) looms large in the popular imagination surrounding Onmyodo. He is often portrayed as a divinely gifted Onmyoji, possessing extraordinary powers and wisdom. Legends attribute his birth to a kitsune (fox spirit) mother, granting him inherent magical abilities. Stories depict him effortlessly manipulating spirits, predicting the future with uncanny accuracy, and vanquishing demons with ease. One famous tale recounts how he discovered that a court noble was plotting treason by observing the unusual movements of birds in the sky. Another story tells of his ability to conjure shikigami, invisible spirits that served as his eyes and ears, allowing him to gather information and protect himself from harm. 

Beyond Abe no Seimei, other Onmyoji have also been featured in folklore and literature. Ashiya Doman, often depicted as Seimei's rival, is portrayed as a cunning and ambitious practitioner who sought to usurp Seimei's position and power. Stories often pit the two against each other in magical duels, showcasing their respective skills and abilities. While Seimei is generally portrayed as benevolent and righteous, Doman is often depicted as malevolent and driven by selfish desires. These stories, while fictionalized, reflect the competitive nature of the courtly environment and the potential for power struggles within the Onmyodo community.

These narratives have been adapted and reinterpreted in various forms of media, including kabuki plays, novels, manga, and anime. These adaptations often embellish the original legends, adding new characters, plotlines, and magical techniques. For instance, the popular series *Onmyoji* features Abe no Seimei as a young and charismatic protagonist who battles demons and protects the capital city of Heian-kyo. These contemporary adaptations have helped to keep the legacy of Onmyodo alive and introduce it to new generations of audiences, both in Japan and abroad. They often explore themes of fate, destiny, and the balance between the human and spiritual realms.

extendedContent.title: Significance and Cultural Legacy
extendedContent.content:
Onmyodo's influence extends far beyond its historical role as a system of divination and magic. It has deeply permeated Japanese culture, shaping beliefs, customs, and artistic expressions. The concepts of Yin and Yang and the Five Elements are still widely understood and applied in various aspects of life, from traditional medicine to martial arts. The influence of Onmyodo can be seen in the layout of cities, the design of gardens, and the practice of feng shui (geomancy). Many traditional Japanese festivals and ceremonies incorporate elements of Onmyodo, such as purification rituals and the use of talismans and amulets. 

The legacy of Onmyodo is also evident in Japanese literature, theater, and visual arts. Characters and themes related to Onmyodo frequently appear in historical dramas, ghost stories, and fantasy narratives. The figure of the Onmyoji, with their mystical powers and enigmatic aura, has become a popular archetype in Japanese popular culture. The continued interest in Onmyodo reflects a fascination with the supernatural and a desire to connect with the ancient traditions of Japan. The image of Abe no Seimei, in particular, continues to captivate audiences, inspiring countless works of art and entertainment. 

While Onmyodo is no longer practiced as an official institution, its influence continues to be felt in contemporary Japanese society. Some individuals still practice Onmyodo privately, offering divination services and performing rituals for those seeking guidance or protection. The continued popularity of Onmyodo-related media and merchandise suggests a enduring fascination with this esoteric tradition. The integration of Onmyodo into modern narratives and artistic expressions ensures that its legacy will continue to evolve and adapt to the changing cultural landscape of Japan.

shortDescription: Onmyodo is a syncretic Japanese esoteric cosmology and magic system influenced by Chinese Wu Xing and Yin-Yang, integrated with Shinto.
